figures.caption: Figure 2: Image inpainting comparison. The leftmost panel shows incomplete images at different rates. The second and third panels show four columns with conditional samples produced by RePaint and MO-ARM, respectively, with their averages in the fifth columns. The fourth panel shows the ground truth image. The rightmost panel reports average PSNR as a function of the missing percentage.
Figure 5: Per-dataset average test imputation RMSE of MCAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across five missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.
Figure 6: Per-dataset average test imputation MAE of MCAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across ten missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.

Figure 3: Average test imputation performance at a 50% missing rate across the nine UCI tabular benchmarks. The horizontal axis denotes the training missing rate. For the accuracy metric, results are reported on the subset of four datasets containing categorical features. The top row corresponds to MCAR missingness, while the bottom row corresponds to MNAR missingness.
Figure 8: Per-dataset average test imputation RMSE of MNAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across five missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.
Figure 7: Per-dataset average test imputation accuracy of MCAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across ten missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.
Figure 4: Sequential active information acquisition with MO-ARM and baselines on UCI datasets.
Figure 9: Per-dataset average test imputation MAE of MNAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across ten missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.
Figure 10: Per-dataset average test imputation accuracy of MNAR missing data at a 50% missing rate across ten missing masks. The horizontal axis indicates the training missing rate.

Figure 1: Graphical models used throughout the paper. Boxes denote collections of variables. The left panel illustrates the OA-ARM formulation, where latent variables z = (z1, . . . , zL) define a permutation over the L elements and observed variables x = (xz1 , . . . ,xzL) are generated autoregressively. The right panel shows the missingness-aware inference model used for imputation.
Table 1: Statistics of datasets. # Num and # Cat denote the number of numerical and categorical columns, respectively, reported as feature plus target columns when target features are included.
